name: Deer Trail Mountain
short_description: BLM rockhounding on the northern Colorado Plateau near Piute County. Find colorful agate, quartz, and jasper specimens in exposed volcanic wash deposits. Free public access, 25 lbs daily limit.
description: Deer Trail Mountain sits on productive BLM land in south-central Utah's high desert, an area rich with volcanic and sedimentary mineral deposits. The location offers accessible rockhounding for collectors seeking quality agate, jasper, and quartz specimens typical of the Colorado Plateau province. Collectors report finding colorful banded agates and translucent quartz fragments in washes and along exposed ridges where water runoff has concentrated lighter materials. The terrain combines open shrubland with volcanic outcrop, making for pleasant hiking and productive collecting in a relatively remote but accessible setting. This is a solid destination for collectors of all levels who want free public land access without the crowds of commercial pay-to-dig sites.
history: Deer Trail Mountain lies within the Colorado Plateau's transition zone, where Paleozoic and Mesozoic sedimentary rocks were interlaced with Tertiary volcanic activity. Silica-rich hydrothermal fluids deposited during these volcanic episodes formed the agate and chalcedony bodies found here today, while weathering and erosion of surrounding formations concentrated collectable specimens in accessible washes and dry arroyos.
tips: Search the exposed washes and drainage channels where water has concentrated lighter stone and minerals - these areas typically yield the best specimens. Focus on hillsides with visible volcanic outcrops and reddish or tan-colored soil patches, as agates often weather out in these zones. Bring a small magnifying glass to inspect banding patterns and color in potential pieces before collecting. Early morning light makes spotting translucent quartz and agate much easier among the surrounding tan and gray rocks.
terrain_notes: Open high desert shrubland at approximately 8,500 feet elevation. Gently rolling to moderately steep slopes with volcanic rock outcrops. Sparse juniper and pinyon pine with patches of desert scrub. Footing ranges from firm volcanic hardpan to loose scree on steeper sections. Expect uneven, rocky terrain requiring sturdy boots. Walking distances from parking typically range from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on target areas.
best_season: May - October. Summer heat can be intense at midday, so early morning starts are recommended. Winter snow occasionally closes access to higher sections. Late spring and early fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions with stable weather and moderate temperatures.
written_directions: From Marysvale, Utah (approximately 20 miles north), take Utah State Route 118 south toward Piute County. Continue toward the Deer Trail Mountain area as indicated by BLM signage. Access points are typically marked by informal pullouts along public land boundaries. Use a GPS unit or mapping application to navigate to the coordinates 38.383826, -112.307814. Local BLM office in Salina can provide detailed current road conditions and access updates.
road_conditions: Unpaved roads, likely washboard texture. High-clearance passenger vehicle recommended but not required. Check with Salina BLM office before wet weather. Approach roads may require 4WD after rain or snow.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and turn-arounds along BLM access roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical roadside parking areas. Arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of archaeological artifacts or fossils. Respect posted claim boundaries. Leave area clean - pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation
No fossils or archaeological artifacts
Respect mining claim signs
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Marysvale, Utah (approximately 20 miles north) offers gas, food, and water. Salina BLM office provides maps and current conditions.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose volcanic scree on steeper slopes. Moderate elevation around 8,500 feet may affect those unaccustomed to higher altitudes. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Not suitable for limited mobility. Hiking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles from parking depending on target area.

faq.question: What types of agates and stones can you find at Deer Trail Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: Deer Trail Mountain is known for colorful banded agates, jasper specimens, translucent quartz fragments, and chalcedony. Most specimens are found in washes and along volcanic outcrops where water has concentrated lighter materials. Colors range from tan and cream to red, rust, and gray tones typical of Colorado Plateau volcanic deposits.

faq.question: Is Deer Trail Mountain free rockhounding, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Deer Trail Mountain is completely free rockhounding on BLM public land with no permit required. You only need to follow FLPMA regulations: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, and no commercial collection. This makes it an excellent choice for collectors who want to avoid pay-to-dig site fees.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Deer Trail Mountain near Marysvale?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best conditions. Avoid winter snow and summer midday heat by visiting in late spring or early fall. Early morning starts help you beat the heat and maximize collecting time in this high desert location at 8,500 feet elevation.

faq.question: How difficult is Deer Trail Mountain for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Deer Trail Mountain is moderate difficulty and beginner-friendly. Hiking distances range from 0.5 to 2 miles from parking, and kids ages 8 and up can participate comfortably with proper footwear and hydration. The open terrain and visible mineral deposits make it easy to spot collectible specimens without advanced skills.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring when rockhounding at Deer Trail Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for uneven rocky terrain, a small magnifying glass to inspect agate banding and quartz clarity, a collecting bag or backpack for specimens, plenty of water (at least 2-3 liters), sun protection, and a GPS unit or map showing BLM boundaries. A small chisel may help extract loose pieces from exposed deposits.

faq.question: How far is Deer Trail Mountain from nearby towns, and where can you get supplies?
faq.answer: Marysvale is the nearest town at approximately 20 miles north, offering gas stations, restaurants, and markets. The Salina BLM office nearby provides current maps, road conditions, and access information. Plan to fill up on water and supplies in Marysvale before driving out, as services are not available at the collecting site.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Marysvale. Find agate, jasper, and quartz. No permit, 25 lbs daily limit. Beginner-friendly Colorado Plateau site.
